revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:安国寺 (茅野市)]]
{{Other uses|the temple in Chino City, Nagano Prefecture|other temples named Ankoku-ji|Ankoku-ji|qq=}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Ankoku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|1=Rinzai school}} |image = {{image requested|cat=Chino City|qq=}} | caption = | location = 2819 Miyagawa Ankokuji, Chino City, Nagano Prefecture, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 59 | lats = 19.3 | latNS = N | longd = 138 |longm = 8 | longs = 27.2 | longEW = E | map_code = JP-20 | mount = Taihei-zan | sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji School of Rinzai Zen Buddhism|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji School of Rinzai Zen Buddhism|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q108821667}} | deity = Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha | established = [[:en:Ryakuo|Ryakuo]] 4 (1341) | founder = | restoration = [[:en:Anei|Anei]] era | grounds = | website = | map2 = {{Switcher |{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|frame-align=center|type=point|zoom=11|frame-width=250|frame-height=180|frame-latitude=36.015|frame-longitude=138.136|qq=}} |Wikimedia maps |{{location map|Japan Nagano Prefecture|relief=1|float=center|width=250|qq=}} |Nagano map |qq=}} | prefecture= {{ill|Chino|qid=Q838660|1=Chino, Nagano}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.98869444444444|N|138.1408888888889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Ankoku-ji''' (安国寺) or '''Ankoku Zenji''' is a temple of the {{ill|Myoshin-ji School of Rinzai Zen Buddhism|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji School of Rinzai Zen Buddhism|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q108821667}} located in {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|qid=Q838660|lt=Chino|1=Chino, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture, Japan. It is situated in the Ankokuji area of Miyagawa and is dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is Taihei-zan. Ankoku-ji was founded during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} as one of the {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|qid=Q10158536|lt=Ankoku-ji|1=Ankoku-ji}}, a network of temples established by the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|qid=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga clan|1=Ashikaga clan}}. == History == The temple was founded in {{ill|Ryakuō|en|Ryakuō|qid=Q1146182|lt=Ryakuō|1=Ryakuō}} 4 (1341) as part of the {{ill|Ankoku-ji|en|Ankoku-ji|qid=Q10158536|lt=Ankoku-ji|1=Ankoku-ji}}, a network of temples built during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and his brother {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|qid=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}. Its establishment in {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} was facilitated by {{ill|Suwa Enchu|ja|諏訪円忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Enchu|1=Suwa Enchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20040902}}, an official of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}. The original temple featured a full set of seven halls (''shichidō garan'') and included eight sub-temples (''tatchū''), such as Eikan-ji, Todō-ji, Nōkun-ji, Onkō-ji, Shōgan-ji, Sanshō-ji, Kongō-ji, and Taishō-ji. It was located near the {{ill|Mae-miya|ja|諏訪大社前宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mae-miya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the shrine associated with the {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} Upper Shrine. The area around the temple developed as a temple town, with neighborhoods such as Higashi-Ōmachi and Nishi-Ōmachi forming nearby. In {{ill|Bunmei|en|Bunmei|qid=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 12 (1480), the temple was damaged by fire during conflicts between the Upper and Lower Shrines of Suwa. Two years later, a major flood of the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Tenryū River basin)|ja|宮川 (天竜川水系)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagawa River (Tenryū River basin)|1=Miya River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q115992478}} washed away much of the temple complex, leaving it in ruins. In {{ill|Tenbun|en|Tenbun|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 11 (1542), the temple suffered further destruction during the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17214872}}. During the [[:en:Anei|Anei]] era, the temple was relocated and rebuilt at its current site, with support from the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|qid=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}, and was affiliated with the {{ill|Myōshin-ji|en|Myōshin-ji|qid=Q376288|lt=Myoshin-ji School|1=Myōshin-ji}}. The temple's crest (''kamon'') combines the {{ill|Ashikaga clan's double-bar crest|ja|足利二つ引|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga clan's double-bar crest|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Suwa clan's kaji-no-ha crest|ja|梶の葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa clan's kaji-no-ha crest|1=Suwa clan's kaji-no-ha crest|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11538786}}. === City-Designated Historic Site === The temple grounds include the "Suwa Clan Ankoku-ji Mausoleum," designated as a city historic site. The mausoleum contains gravestones and {{ill|Hōkyōintō|en|Hōkyōintō|qid=Q3146365|lt=Hōkyōintō|1=Hōkyōintō}} inscribed with the posthumous names of {{ill|Suwa Mitsuchika|ja|諏訪満隣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Mitsuchika|1=Suwa Mitsuchika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11631904}} and his wife, as well as a memorial for his brother, {{ill|Suwa Yorikaga|ja|諏訪頼隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorikaga|1=Suwa Yoritaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11631967}}. The mausoleum received regular upkeep and funding from the Suwa Domain during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Although a typhoon in 1959 damaged the structure, many elements remain intact.<ref>Historical marker for the Suwa Clan Ankoku-ji Mausoleum, Chino City.</ref> Close to the mausoleum is the "Hisawa Castle Ruins," also a city-designated historic site. Hisawa Castle, also called Ankoku-ji Castle, played a significant role in defending the nearby Mae-miya Shrine.<ref>Historical marker for the Hisawa Castle Ruins, Chino City.</ref> == Grounds == * Main Hall * Bell Tower == Access == * Five minutes by car from JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|en|Chūō Main Line|qid=Q1078110|lt=Chūō Main Line|1=Chūō Main Line}} {{ill|Chino Station|en|Chino Station|qid=Q844063|lt=Chino Station|1=Chino Station}} * Ten minutes by car from the {{ill|Chūō Expressway|en|Chūō Expressway|qid=Q1091276|lt=Chūō Expressway|1=Chūō Expressway}} Suwa Interchange == Related Sites == * {{ill|Jinnchōkan Moriya Historical Museum|ja|神長官守矢史料館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinnchōkan Moriya Historical Museum|1=Jinchōkan Moriya Historical Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591010}} (nearby) * {{ill|Miyagawa Village (Nagano Prefecture)|ja|宮川村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagawa Village (Nagano Prefecture)|1=Miyagawa Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11454702}} * [[Chino City Temples and Shrines]] == References == * ''Temples and Shrines of Shinshu'' (1981), Shinano Mainichi Shimbunsha * ''Exploring Shinshu’s Zen Temples'' (1996), Kyōdo Shuppansha {{ISBN2|978-4876633258|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://www.city.chino.lg.jp/soshiki/bunkazai/1665.html Chino City Homepage - City-Designated Cultural Properties] == Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Suwa Faith|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|安国寺 (茅野市)|version=101724230|comment=Imported full ja history}}
